one_line_takeaway: ORACAL fuses CFG/DFG/CallGraph structure with LLM-RAG-generated security annotations and a causal dual-branch attention mechanism, reaching 91.28% Macro F1 on SoliAudit (vs. up to 82.63% for the best prior SOTA, MANDO-HGT) while cutting adversarial Attack Success Rate to ~3% versus up to 18.73% for the strongest baseline, SCVHunter.
key_finding: On the SoliAudit in-domain test set, the best ORACAL configuration (Causal training + Only Edge Enrichment) reaches 91.28% Macro F1 vs. 87.19% for the equivalent Standard-trained model (a +4.09pp gain from causal training alone); causal training improves the no-enrichment baseline from 86.94% to 89.55%. An enrichment-text-only ablation (dropping the graph structure entirely) performs poorly (72.21% Standard / 74.75% Causal Macro F1), showing enrichment cannot substitute for the structural graph backbone. On OOD generalization, ORACAL-edge Causal reaches 0.918 Macro F1 on CGT Weakness, 0.771 on DAppScan, and 0.813 on LLMAV, each significantly above its Standard-trained counterpart (Wilcoxon p=0.0625, Vargha-Delaney A12=1.00 in all three comparisons, i.e. every causal run beat every standard run across seeds). Against SOTA baselines (Table 8, 'Original' pre-attack setting), ORACAL-node-edge Causal achieves 90.48% F1 (SoliAudit), 90.83% (CGTWeakness), and 72.82% (DAppScan) versus MANDO-HGT's 82.63%/83.77%/62.00%, MTVHunter's 78.10%/78.73%/58.94%, and SCVHunter's 51.62%/50.20%/28.44% (SCVHunter collapses on the real-world DAppScan set). Under HSAttack (10% edge budget), ORACAL-node-edge Causal degrades only 2.35pp F1 on SoliAudit (ASR 2.94%) versus SCVHunter's 18.58pp degradation (ASR 18.73%) and MANDO-HGT's 9.69pp degradation (ASR 12.41%); under SubAttack it degrades 1.11-1.84pp (ASR ~2%); under combined structural+textual attack it degrades 4.57pp on SoliAudit (still smaller than any single-attack degradation on the SOTA baselines). PGExplainer is the best of three tested explainers, reaching 40.91% MSP / 44.85% MSR / 32.51% MIoU on LLMAV and 39.68%/42.77%/30.85% on DAppScan, beating GNNExplainer by 4.55-5.13pp on each metric and substantially beating AttentionExplainer (19.17% MIoU on LLMAV). A qualitative case study on an Unchecked-Low-Level-Calls contract shows PGExplainer correctly isolating all three unchecked call sites with minimal false positives, while GNNExplainer and AttentionExplainer over-flag structurally salient but semantically irrelevant lines (e.g., pragma directive, a view function).
core_contribution: ORACAL is a heterogeneous multimodal graph-learning framework for Solidity smart-contract vulnerability detection that (1) fuses Control Flow Graph, Data Flow Graph, and Call Graph representations into a single heterogeneous contract graph with GraphCodeBERT node embeddings, (2) selects a connected top-k 'critical' subgraph via a hybrid importance score (GAT attention + k-core + PageRank + Louvain community/betweenness centrality) and enriches it via a RAG pipeline (LangChain orchestration, Milvus vector store, Google Gemini 3 Flash generation over a corpus of Solidity docs, EVM specs, the SWC registry, and audit datasets, with BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5 embeddings and bge-reranker-v2-m3 re-ranking) that produces structured JSON node/edge annotations (semantic_meaning, operational_context, security_analysis, edge_relationship), and (3) trains a dual-branch 'CausalAttentionHeteroClassifier' that separately encodes RAG-enriched ('causal') features and raw GraphCodeBERT ('spurious') features, fuses them via learned attention weights, and is optimized with a 4-term multi-task loss (supervised BCE on the causal branch, a uniformity loss pushing the spurious branch to 0.5, a backdoor-adjustment/do-calculus loss using batch-shuffled spurious representations, and a contrastive margin loss separating the two representations) to disentangle genuine vulnerability signals from spurious structural/syntactic correlations. Explanations are produced via PGExplainer at the subgraph level.
threat_model: Not an attack-on-a-system threat model in the offensive-security sense; the paper's 'adversarial' setting is a black-box hard-label evasion setting against the vulnerability detector itself. Three representative attacks are used: HSAttack (structural edge addition/removal within CFG/DFG, edge budget 0.1, i.e. up to 10% of edges, applied to heterogeneous models: ORACAL, SCVHunter, MANDO-HGT), CAMA (node-importance-guided structural attack applied to homogeneous models GNN-SC and MTVHunter), and SubAttack (textual antonym substitution on enriched semantic text, keyword budget 10 words/node, applied to ORACAL's text-enriched variants). All three assume no access to model internals (hard-label black-box). The paper does not model attacks on the RAG pipeline itself (e.g., corpus poisoning) or on the underlying blockchain/contract execution.
limitations: Authors' own Threats-to-Validity section: (1) preprocessing/AST-extraction reliance may bias datasets toward well-structured contracts (large fractions of raw contracts were dropped — e.g., only 124 of 1,199 DAppScan reports and 1,345 of 3,103 CGT Weakness contracts survived graph extraction); (2) RAG enrichment quality depends on corpus composition and LLM stochasticity, only partially controlled via fixed seeds and zero temperature; (3) generalization evaluation used only the single top-performing checkpoint per training paradigm rather than exhaustively re-evaluating all ablation variants OOD; (4) findings are Solidity/DASP-Top-10-specific and may not transfer to other languages or to newer vulnerability classes like flash loans or MEV; (5) the model is trained on static snapshots and subject to temporal concept drift as the Solidity ecosystem evolves; (6) adversarial robustness was evaluated at a single fixed perturbation budget (10% edges / 10 keywords) rather than across a budget sweep, so the full robustness profile against stronger or differently-constrained adversaries is unknown; (7) OOD test sets are small (DAppScan n=124 contracts), and Wilcoxon tests over only 5 seeds yield p-values bounded below by the test's discrete resolution (minimum achievable p=0.0625 given n=5), so significance claims lean on the A12 effect size rather than p<0.05. The paper itself flags future work (line-level/node-classification localization, systematic faithfulness evaluation of RAG-generated text, incremental learning) as unaddressed by the current work.

datasets_used: SoliAudit (10,655 valid contracts after preprocessing) - training/in-domain test
CGT Weakness (1,345 contracts after graph conversion, from 3,103 original) - training/in-domain test
DAppScan (124 contracts processed from 1,199 real audit reports) - OOD test only
LLMAV (1,029 unique contracts after dedup from 2,081; line-level DASP-Top-10 annotations) - OOD test and explainability evaluation only
quantitative_results: Best in-domain Macro F1: 91.28% (Causal + Only Edge Enrichment) on SoliAudit vs. 87.19% Standard equivalent
Causal no-enrichment baseline: 89.55% vs Standard no-enrichment baseline 86.94%
OOD Macro F1 (ORACAL-edge Causal): CGT Weakness 0.918, DAppScan 0.771, LLMAV 0.813 (all vs. Standard counterparts with Vargha-Delaney A12=1.00, Wilcoxon p=0.0625)
SOTA comparison Macro F1 (ORACAL-node-edge Causal): SoliAudit 90.48%, CGTWeakness 90.83%, DAppScan 72.82%

Adversarial: HSAttack degrades ORACAL-node-edge Causal by only 2.35pp F1 on SoliAudit (ASR 2.94%); SubAttack degrades 1.11-1.84pp F1 (ASR ~2%); combined attack degrades 4.57pp (ASR 5.71%) on SoliAudit
Explainability (PGExplainer): 40.91% MSP / 44.85% MSR / 32.51% MIoU on LLMAV; 39.68%/42.77%/30.85% on DAppScan
Inference time: 1.32-1.90 ms/sample depending on enrichment scenario (all under 2ms)
ROC-AUC: ORACAL-node-edge Causal 0.96 on SoliAudit/CGTWeakness, 0.76 on DAppScan, vs SCVHunter 0.73 and 0.39 respectively
baselines_compared: GNNExplainer and AttentionExplainer (as explainability baselines vs. PGExplainer)
